Re-appointment of Mr. Niten Malhan (DIN: 00614624) as Non-Executive Independent Director of the Company

Mr. Niten Malhan (DIN: 00614624) was appointed as Independent Director on the Board of the Company pursuant to the provisions of Section 149 of the Companies Act, 2013 (“the Act”) read with the Companies (Appointment and Qualification of Directors) Rules, 2014 and relevant regulations of SEBI (LODR) Regulations, 2015, by the Shareholders at the 29th Annual General Meeting held on September 30, 2021 for a period of 5 (five) years i.e. from November 06, 2020 upto November 05, 2025 (“first term” in accordance with the explanation to Section 149 of the Act).

Mr. Niten Malhan is the founder and managing partner of New Mark Advisors LLP, an investment manager focused on applying the principles of private equity investing to small and mid-sized listed Indian companies with an aim to achieve long term compounding of capital. Prior to founding New Mark Advisors LLP in April 2018, he was the managing director and co-head of India at Warburg Pincus India Private Limited (“Warburg Pincus”), a global private equity firm. Mr. Niten Malhan joined Warburg Pincus in 2001 and became a partner at the firm in 2007. In 2012, he was appointed the co-head of the India business, co-leading a team of 15 investment professionals and a portfolio of over $3 billion in value. Between 2012 and 2017, Niten was also a member of the global executive management group of the firm, a group of senior partners who lead different offices and industry groups at Warburg Pincus.

Prior to joining Warburg Pincus, he worked as director of business development at Stratum 8, a Silicon Valley technology start-up company. Before that, he was an engagement manager at McKinsey & Company, and worked in the India, South East Asia and Boston offices of the firm.

Mr. Niten studied Computer Science & Engineering at Indian Institute of Technology, New Delhi, and completed his Post Graduate Diploma in Management from Indian Institute of Management, Ahmedabad. Niten is a Founder and Trustee of Plaksha University
and has also served as the Vice Chairman of the Indian Private Equity and Venture Capital Association.

The Nomination and Remuneration Committee at its meeting held on August 08, 2025 on the basis of performance evaluation of Independent Directors and taking into account the external business environment, the business knowledge, acumen, experience and the substantial contribution made by Mr. Niten Malhan during his tenure, has recommended to the Board that continued association of Mr. Niten Malhan as Independent Director of the Company would be beneficial to the Company. Based on the above and the performance evaluation of Independent Directors, the Board recommended the re-appointment of Mr. Niten Malhan, as Independent Director of the Company, not liable to retire by rotation, to hold office for a second term of five consecutive years on the Board of the Company commencing from November 06, 2025 upto November 05, 2030.

A copy of the draft letter of appointment of Mr. Niten Malhan setting out terms and conditions of appointment is available for inspection by the Members at the Registered office of the Company between 3:00 P.M. to 5:00 P.M., on all working days (except Saturdays, Sundays and Public Holidays), up to the date of this Annual General Meeting (AGM).

Mr. Niten Malhan is not disqualified from being appointed as Director in terms of Section 164 of the Companies Act, 2013 and has given his consent to act as an Independent Director. The Company has received declaration from Mr. Niten Malhan stating that he meets the criteria of independence as prescribed under Sub-section (6) of Section 149 of the Companies Act, 2013 and under Rule 6 of the Companies (Appointment and Qualifications of Directors) Rules, 2014 and SEBI (Listing Obligations and Disclosure Requirements) Regulations, 2015 (“Listing Regulations”). He also declared that he is not debarred from holding the office of Directors by virtue of any SEBI order or any other such authority and confirmed that he has registered himself with the Independent Directors’ databank.
In the opinion of the Board, Mr. Niten Malhan fulfils the conditions for appointment as Independent Director as specified in the Companies Act, 2013

and the Listing Regulations and he is independent of the management. Save and except Mr. Niten Malhan and his relatives to the extent of their shareholding interest, if any, in the Company, none of the other Directors, Key Managerial Personnel (KMP) of the Company and their relatives are, in any way, concerned or interested, financially or otherwise, in the Resolution set out under Item No. 13 of the accompanying Notice.

The Board recommends the Special Resolution set out under Item No. 13 of the accompanying Notice for approval of the Members. Disclosure relating to Directors pursuant to SEBI (Listing Obligations and Disclosure Requirements) Regulations, 2015 and Secretarial Standards on General Meetings are given under Annexure A to the Notice.

Amendment in LTHL Stock Appreciation Rights Scheme-2024 (“the Scheme”) and approval of the extension of grant of Stock Appreciation Rights to the employees of existing unlisted Subsidiary Companies of the Company under the Scheme
The Company adopted the LTHL Stock Appreciation Rights Scheme -2024 (“the Scheme”) which was approved by the members at the Annual General Meeting held on September 26, 2024.

Based on the approval of the Nomination and Remuneration Committee (“NRC”), Board of Directors (“Board”), and subject to the approval of the Members, it is proposed to amend the Scheme as per Securities and Exchange Board of India (Share Based Employee Benefits and Sweat Equity) Regulations, 2021 (“SEBI (SBEB & SE) Regulations”).

In view of above and in pursuant to Section 62(1) (b) of Companies Act, 2013 and Regulation 6 & 7 of SEBI (SBEB & SE) Regulations, separate approval of Members is sought by way of a Special Resolution for Item No. 14 and 15, for amending the Scheme and do all such acts, matters, deeds and things and to take all steps and do all things and give such directions as may be required, necessary, expedient, incidental or desirable for giving effect to the amendments of the Scheme.
Details of the key variations in the Scheme along with rationale for amendments are provided below;
The definition of Corporate Action as mentioned in the scheme has been widened to include the situation of merger, demerger, arrangement or any other re-organisation.

The definition of Employee and Eligible Employee as mentioned in the Scheme has been aligned with the clarification made to the stock exchange which include the employee of Lemon Tree Hotels Limited and its existing unlisted subsidiaries.
Appreciation value is being contemplated to be paid fully in the form of shares only instead of part payment in cash. Payment in cash to be done in case of fraction entitlements only.
Further, changes to settle the appreciation in Shares only and only fractional entitlement settled in cash wherever required under the Scheme.
Appreciation value is being contemplated to be paid fully in the form of shares only instead of part payment in cash. Payment in cash to be done in case of fraction entitlements only.
Further, changes to settle the appreciation in Shares only and only fractional entitlement settled in cash wherever required under the Scheme.
Appreciation value is being contemplated to be paid fully in the form of shares only instead of part payment in cash. Payment in cash to be done in case of fraction entitlements only.
Further, changes to settle the appreciation in Shares only and only fractional entitlement settled in cash wherever required under the Scheme.
The definition of Subsidiary has been revised to include only unlisted subsidiary to bring it into more conformity with clause 4.1.13.

The Company is proposing to increase number of SAR Units to be issued up to 1,50,00,000 and shares to be allotted, pursuant to exercise of vested SAR up to 80,00,000 in order to enable the grant of more SAR/shares to the employees in order to cover the larger number of employees including new senior managerial positions (As mentioned in Item No. 8 and 10 of the Notice).
As per the amended scheme, the tenure of employment of an Employee/ Grantee in the Company and its unlisted existing subsidiaries, considered for considering the eligibility for Grant.
Milestones for vesting of SAR units defined herein are now based on time period and are linked to the achievement of Benchmark market price which is now defined as a multiple of SAR Price.
No fraction of any vested SAR Units can be exercise under the Scheme.
Appreciation value is being contemplated to be paid fully in the form of shares only instead of part payment in cash.

All SAR units (whether vested or unvested) as on date of death would vest immediately in the Legal Heirs / Nominee of the Grantee on that day.
All SAR units (whether vested or unvested) as on date of Permanent Disability would vest immediately in the Legal Heirs / Nominee of the Grantee on that day.
New Clause inserted - Change has been incorporated in order to cover the event of the death of the employee post retirement.

This provision is aligned with the definition of Employee and Eligible Employee as mentioned in the Scheme has aligned with the clarification made to the stock exchange which include the employee of Lemon Tree Hotels Limited and its existing unlisted subsidiary.
Details of the employees who are beneficiaries of such variation:

All Eligible employees as per the Scheme to whom the SAR Units will be granted along with respective approvals under the Scheme. However, as on date no SAR units have been granted to the employees, hence the proposed amendments are not detrimental to the interests of the employees who are beneficiaries under the scheme.

In terms of Section 62(1)(b) of the Companies Act, 2013 read with Regulation 6 & 7 of the Securities and Exchange Board of India (Share Based Employee Benefits and Sweat Equity) Regulations, 2021 [“SEBI (SBEB & SE) Regulations, 2021”] the Company seeks your approval for implementation of the Scheme and grant SAR Units thereunder to the eligible employees as per the Scheme.
Features of the proposed Scheme are specified under:

The Scheme seeks to reward Eligible employees by way of granting stock appreciation rights, with a view to reward their association and loyalty which has resulted in corporate growth and value creation over a long period of time.
This Scheme shall be called the “LTHL -Stock Appreciation Rights Scheme – 2024” hereinafter referred as “the Scheme”.

The Company proposes to implement the Scheme through Direct Route for extending benefits to Employees wherein the Company will distribute in the form of Shares in accordance with the Scheme. The Scheme shall continue to be in effect up to 8 years from the effective date of the scheme unless terminated earlier by the Board of Directors.

The Committee may on the basis of all or any of the following criteria, decide on the Employees / Grantees who are eligible for the grant / vesting of SAR Units under the Scheme and the terms and conditions thereof.
• Loyalty: To be determined on the basis of tenure of employment of an Employee / Grantee in the Company and its existing Subsidiary.
• Performance of Employee / Grantee: Performance during the financial year on the basis of the parameters decided by the Board of Directors or Committee.
• Performance of Company: Performance of the Company as per the standards set by the Board of Directors or Committee.
Any other criteria as decided by the Committee in consultation with Board of Directors from time to time.
b) Total number of SARs to be granted:

The maximum number of SAR Units that may be issued pursuant to this Scheme shall not exceed 1,50,00,000 and the maximum number of Shares that may be issued and allotted pursuant to exercise of SAR Units shall not exceed 80,00,000 at a face value of H 10 each.

If any SAR Unit granted under the Scheme lapses or is forfeited or surrendered under any provision of the Scheme, such SAR Unit shall be added back to the pool and shall be available for further grant under the Scheme unless otherwise determined by the Committee.
Further, the maximum number of SAR Units that can be granted and the Shares arise upon exercise of these SAR Units shall stand adjusted in case of corporate action, as defined in the Scheme.
c) Identification of classes of employees entitled to participate and be Beneficiaries in the Scheme:
(i) an employee as designated by the company, who is exclusively working in India or outside India; or

(ii) a director of the company, whether a whole- time director or not, including a nonexecutive director who is not a promoter or member of the promoter group, but excluding an independent director; or
(iii) an employee as defined in sub-clauses (i) or (ii), of the existing unlisted subsidiaries Company, in India or outside India of the Company, but does not include—
(a) an employee who is a promoter or a person belonging to the promoter group; or
(b) a director who, either himself or through his relative or through anybody corporate, directly or indirectly, holds more than ten per cent of the outstanding equity shares of the company;
“Eligible Employee” means those employees who have been identified as Eligible on meeting the eligibility criteria set by the Nomination and Remuneration Committee.
“Eligibility Criteria” means the criteria, as may be determined from time to time by the Committee, for grant or vesting of SAR Units.
d) Requirements of vesting and period of vesting:

Vesting period shall commence after minimum 2 (Two) years from the grant date and shall not exceed the maximum period of 8 (Eight) years from the effective date of the Scheme, subject to achievement of milestones as mentioned below.
1st Milestone Achieving Benchmark Market Price equivalent to 1.67x of SAR Price
2nd Milestone Achieving Benchmark Market Price equivalent to 2.00x of SAR Price
3rd Milestone Achieving Benchmark Market Price equivalent to 2.34 x of SAR Price
4th Milestone Achieving Benchmark Market Price equivalent to 2.67x of SAR Price
The vesting Schedules upon achievement of Milestone shall be as follows:
Milestone to be achieved % of SAR Units to be vested
At the achievement of First Milestone 25% of the SAR Units granted
At the achievement of Second Milestone 25% of the SAR Units granted
At the achievement of Third Milestone 25% of the SAR Units granted
At the achievement of Fourth Milestone 25% of the SAR Units granted

“Benchmark Market price” means the average of weekly average price of 4 weeks price (Closing) on a recognised stock exchange on which the Shares of the Company are listed on the date immediately prior to the relevant date.
e) Maximum period within which the SAR shall be vested
Vesting period shall not exceed the maximum period of 8 (Eight) years from the effective date of the Scheme, subject to achievement of milestones as mentioned in clause (d) above.

Upon vesting of SAR Units, the Committee in accordance with SEBI (SBEB & SE) Regulations and terms of grant, shall calculate the Appreciation occurred on such SAR Units. The Appreciation value in this case will be based on the difference between the market price on Respective Vesting Date and SAR Price.

“Vesting Date Price” means the price which is calculated by the Committee at the time of vesting of SAR Units with the Grantees. The Vesting Date Price shall be calculated based upon the market price on respective vesting Date.
For the purpose of the Scheme, SAR Price shall be calculated on the basis of Market price as defined in the Scheme.
“Market price” means the latest available closing price on a recognised stock exchange on which the Shares of the Company are listed on the date immediately prior to the relevant date.
i) In the case of grant, the date of the meeting of the Committee on which the grant is made; or
ii) In the case of vesting, the date of vesting of SAR Units ; or
iii) In the case of exercise, the date on which the notice/letter of exercise is given to the Company by the Grantee.
g) Exercise period/offer period, and process of exercise/ acceptance of Shares:
The vested SAR Units can be exercised by Grantees within the exercise period which shall be 1 (one) year from the vesting date.

The vested SAR Units shall be exercisable either wholly or in part, according to the terms and conditions as determined and mentioned under the vesting letter. However, in case of fraction of a vested SAR Unit, no Unit shall be exercisable.

Upon exercising, the Grantee shall be entitled to receive such number of Equity Shares in lieu of appreciation earned. The number of Equity Shares to be rewarded shall be determined by the Committee, by dividing the Appreciation amount by Exercise Date Price.

In the event of doubt in respect of determination of relevant date and market price, the Committee shall have the discretion, in compliance with SEBI (SBEB & SE) Regulations and terms of grant, to calculate Exercise Date Price, based on the facts relating to each exercise.
h) Appraisal process for determining the eligibility of employees under Scheme

The Committee may on the basis of all or any of the following criteria, decide on the Employees/ Grantees who are eligible for the grant of SAR Units under the Scheme and the terms and conditions thereof.
(a) Loyalty: To be determined on the basis of tenure of employment of an Employee / Grantee in the Company and its existing Subsidiary.
(b) Performance of Employee / Grantee: Performance during the financial year on the basis of the parameters decided by the Board of Directors or Committee.
(c) Performance of Company: Performance of the Company as per the standards set by the Board of Directors or Committee.
Any other criteria as decided by the Committee in consultation with Board of Directors from time to time.
The Employees / Grantees satisfying the eligibility criteria shall be termed as eligible Employee / Grantee.
i) Maximum number of SARs to be offered per Employee and in aggregate:

The maximum number of SAR Units that may be issued pursuant to this Scheme shall not exceed 1,50,00,000 and the maximum number of Shares that may be issued and allotted pursuant to exercise of SAR Units shall not exceed 80,00,000 at a face value of H 10 each.

Subject to availability of SAR Units in the pool under the Scheme, the maximum number of SAR Units that can be granted to any eligible Employee during any one year shall be less than 1% of the issued capital of the Company at the time of grant. The Committee may decide to grant such number of SAR Units equal to or exceeding 1% of the issued capital to any eligible Employee as the case may be, subject to the separate approval of the Shareholders in a general meeting.
j) Maximum quantum of benefits to be provided per employee under the Scheme:

The maximum quantum of benefits that will be provided to every eligible Employee under the Scheme shall be equivalent to the appreciation which shall be the difference between the Vesting Date Price and the SAR Price which shall be settled in equity shares, subject to provision of the Scheme.
k) Whether the Scheme is to be implemented and administered directly by the Company or through a trust:
This Scheme shall be administered by the Company directly through Committee working under the powers delegated by the Board or in accordance with the SEBI Regulations and not through Trust.
l) Whether the Scheme involved new issue of Shares by the Company or secondary acquisition by the Trust or both;
The Scheme shall involve new issuance of Shares.
m) The amount of loan to be provided for implementation of the Scheme by the Company to the Trust, its tenure, utilisation, repayment terms, etc:
Not applicable, since the Scheme is proposed to be implemented by direct route.
n) The maximum percentage of secondary acquisition (subject to limits specified under the regulations) that can be made by the Trust for the purposes of the Scheme:
Not applicable, since the Scheme is proposed to be implemented by direct route.

The Company shall comply with the disclosures requirements and the accounting policies prescribed under Regulation 15 of the SEBI (SBEB & SE) Regulations, 2021 or as may be prescribed by regulatory authorities from time to time.

The Company shall comply with the requirements of IND-AS and shall use Fair Value method. The Company shall make all the necessary disclosures required under the provisions of the SEBI (SBEB & SE) Regulations, 2021 and other Applicable Laws.
q) Statement with regard to Disclosure in Director’s Report:

As the company is adopting fair value method, presently there is no requirement for disclosure in director’s report. However, if in future, the Company opts for expensing of share-based employee benefits using the intrinsic value, then the difference between the employee compensation cost so computed and the employee compensation cost that shall have been recognised if it had used the fair value, shall be disclosed in the Directors’ report and the impact of this difference on profits and on earnings per share (“EPS”) of the company shall also be disclosed in the Directors’ report.
r) Lock In : The Shares allotted to the Grantees pursuant to exercise of SAR Units will not be subject to any lock-in period and can be freely sold by the Grantee.
s) Terms & conditions for buyback, if any, of specified securities:

The Committee has the powers to determine the procedure for buy-back of SAR Units granted under the Scheme, if to be undertaken at any time by the Company, and the applicable terms and conditions, in accordance with the applicable law.

Approval of overall limit of the Commission to the Non-Executive Directors of the Company

The Non-Executive Directors of the Company bring with them significant professional expertise and rich experience across a wide spectrum of functional areas such as business strategy, business development, corporate governance, finance & taxation, risk management.

The Non-Executive Directors are actively involved in various decision-making process and are making valuable contributions towards business development, governance, long-term strategy and compliances. The regulatory requirements, corporate governance norms have been strengthened by the Companies Act, 2013 (“the Act”) and SEBI (Listing Obligations and Disclosure Requirements) Regulations, 2015 with key emphasis on effective governance, risk management, statutory compliances etc. and thereby placing increased accountability on the Board.

The role and responsibilities of the Board particularly the Non-executive Directors have increased more requiring greater time commitments and attention, which reflects in the financial performance. The Board is of the view that it is necessary that adequate compensation be given to the Non-Executive Directors to compensate them for their time and efforts.

Accordingly, it is proposed to pass an ordinary resolution authorising the Board of Directors for payment of Commission to its Non-Executive Directors within the overall limit of 1% of the net profit of the Company to be computed in the manner prescribed in Section 198 of the Companies Act, 2013 in any financial year in pursuance of Section 197 of the Companies Act, 2013 commencing from April 01, 2025 upto March 31, 2030.
The Board recommends the resolution set forth under Item No. 16 for the approval of members as Ordinary Resolution.

All Non-Executive Directors along with their relatives are deemed to be concerned or interested, financially or otherwise in the Resolution at Item No. 16 of the Notice to the extent of the share of commission that may be received by them.

Brief Resume of the Director including Qualification and Experience

Mr. Niten Malhan is the founder and managing partner of New Mark Advisors LLP, an investment manager focused on applying the principles of private equity investing to small and mid-sized listed Indian companies with an aim to achieve long term compounding of capital. Prior to founding New Mark Advisors LLP in April 2018, he was the managing director and co-head of India at Warburg Pincus India Private Limited (“Warburg Pincus”), a global private equity firm. Mr. Niten Malhan joined Warburg Pincus in 2001 and became a partner at the firm in 2007. In 2012, he was appointed the co-head of the India business, co-leading a team of 15 investment professionals and a portfolio of over $3 billion in value. Between 2012 and 2017, Niten was also a member of the global executive management group of the firm, a group of senior partners who lead different offices and industry groups at Warburg Pincus.

Prior to joining Warburg Pincus, he worked as director of business development at Stratum 8, a Silicon Valley technology start-up company. Before that, he was an engagement manager at McKinsey & Company, and worked in the India, South East Asia and Boston offices of the firm. Niten studied Computer Science & Engineering at Indian Institute of Technology, New Delhi, and completed his Post Graduate Diploma in Management from Indian Institute of Management, Ahmedabad. Niten is a Founder and Trustee of Plaksha University and has also served as the Vice Chairman of the Indian Private Equity and Venture Capital Association.

The Company is engaged in the hotel business since 2004. Lemon Tree Hotels Limited (LTHL) is one of the largest hotel chains in India, and owns/ leases/ operates/ franchises hotels across the upscale, upper-midscale, midscale and economy segments. LTHL delivers differentiated yet superior service offerings, with a compelling value proposition. The group offers seven brands to meet guests’ needs across all levels, viz. Aurika Hotels & Resorts, Lemon Tree Premier, Lemon Tree Hotels, Red Fox Hotels by Lemon Tree Hotels, Keys Prima by Lemon Tree Hotels, Keys Select by Lemon Tree Hotels and Keys Lite by Lemon Tree Hotels.

LTHL opened its first hotel with 49 rooms in May 2004. Today, the company has a portfolio of 220+ hotels, which includes over 110 operational hotels and more than 110 hotels set to open in India and internationally. Lemon Tree Hotels are located across metro regions, including the NCR, Mumbai, Kolkata, Bengaluru, Hyderabad and Chennai, as well as numerous tier I, II and III cities such as Pune, Ahmedabad, Chandigarh, Jaipur, Indore, Aurangabad, Udaipur, Visakhapatnam, Kochi, Ludhiana, Thiruvananthapuram, Vijayawada etc. The company expanded internationally with hotels opening in Dubai in December 2019, in Bhutan in February 2020 and in Nepal in April 2024.
2. Date or expected date of commencement of commercial production:

As of March 31, 2025, the Lemon Tree Hotels Group, operates 111 hotels with 10,269 rooms across 70+ destinations in India and overseas. The group also maintains a robust growth pipeline (mainly under management) with 101 hotels and 6,847 rooms under development, offering a strong foundation of operational depth, brand equity, and scalability.

Drawing inspiration from India’s rich cultural heritage and its dynamic economic growth, Lemon Tree Hotels is committed to offering accessible, high-quality hospitality across tier I, II and III cities. We are aligned with the aspirations of India’s rising middle class, providing affordable accommodation that meets the evolving needs of modern travellers. Our expansion is rooted in the country’s diverse culture, empowering local communities while delivering meaningful travel experiences.

At the same time, we remain dedicated to responsible growth. By adopting an asset-light model and embracing sustainable practices, we ensure that our growth is both scalable and environmentally conscious. Our commitment to Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) initiatives, such as reducing water consumption and increasing the use of renewable energy, demonstrates our effort to contribute positively to India’s future. We also prioritise inclusivity by creating employment opportunities for marginalised communities. As we continue to grow, our focus remains on responsible expansion that supports India’s long-term socio-economic development, benefiting all sections of society.
Inspired by India, Growing Responsibly Running Ahead of the Curve

Lemon Tree Hotels Limited is India’s largest mid-market hotel chain with over 110 hotels and 10,000+ rooms across 73 destinations, including metros, tier-II and emerging cities. Founded in 2002, it has grown through a mix of owned, leased, managed and franchised properties, becoming a leader in India’s hospitality sector.

With a multi-brand portfolio spanning economy to upscale segments, Lemon Tree caters to both business and leisure travellers. Its asset-light expansion, digital capabilities and sustainability focus drive scalable and profitable growth. As of FY25, the Company has achieved 85% of its CY28 goal of 20,000 rooms (operational and under- development), showing fast‑paced growth.
Guided by its vision to be India’s preferred mid‑market hotel brand, Lemon Tree remains committed to operational excellence, guest satisfaction and responsible growth.

Our commitment to exceptional service is at the heart of our brand. We prioritise guest comfort, safety and unforgettable experiences, upheld by rigorous standards and continuous staff training. Our Infinity Rewards Loyalty Programme deepens guest engagement through tailored rewards. We nurture a culture of respect, ownership and enjoyment, empowering our teams to deliver memorable stays. Innovation remains a core focus, from digital check-ins to ongoing refurbishment initiatives, all aimed at enhancing guest satisfaction and operational efficiency. These efforts reinforce our leadership in India’s mid-market hospitality sector.

At Lemon Tree Hotels, we focus on expanding our presence through an asset-light model built on management contracts and franchising. This strategy allows us to broaden our reach across diverse destinations quickly. By forging key management and franchise partnerships, we are positioning Lemon Tree Hotels for sustainable and scalable growth in a dynamic sector.

Our extensive portfolio encompasses seven unique brands, thoughtfully designed to serve a wide range of guests, from economy to upscale segments. With a robust presence across India and an expanding international reach, we are committed to offering exceptional value and curated experiences that cater to the diverse needs of our clientele.

Aurika Hotels & Resorts deliver exceptional experiences within sophisticated yet comfortable environments. Each hotel captures the essence of its locale through distinctive design elements and a unique character.

Guests are invited to indulge in innovative dining, engaging entertainment and comprehensive wellness amenities, all paired with attentive service that honours their privacy. Aurika’s philosophy of transcending the ordinary comes to life through inspiring spaces, seamless experiences and state-of- the-art technology that empowers guests to work, play and rejuvenate effortlessly.
We cater to a wide range of guests through seven distinctive brands, from luxury to economy.

Lemon Tree Premier is our upper midscale brand. It enhances the Lemon Tree experience while preserving its hallmark freshness, quirkiness and vibrant energy. These hotels boast stylish, spacious interiors that infuse a sense of elegance for both business and leisure travellers.

Guests indulge in personalised service, premium in-room amenities and award- winning dining options. Facilities include an invigorating swimming pool, a state-of- the-art fitness centre and the rejuvenating Fresco Spa, designed to keep guests feeling as fresh as a lemon. Versatile meeting spaces and a well-equipped business centre ensure seamless productivity for those on the go.

Keys Prima is our premium brand, graciously welcoming guests with chic features and unparalleled service. It provides a distinctive hospitality experience, complete with personalised touches that transform every stay into an unforgettable journey.

Guests are invited to unwind and indulge at the spa, salon or swimming pool and savour a diverse array of cuisines at the brand’s welcoming dining options. With exceptional attention to detail and innovative service, we assure a comfortable and memorable experience for all.
Keys Prima by Lemon Tree Hotels Lemon Tree Premier

Keys Select is our second midscale brand and provides comfort and convenience for both business and leisure travellers alike. These properties offer guests chic interiors, comfortable rooms, convenient business facilities and dependable service. The brand’s signature warm welcome and fond farewell create a cheerful and lively ambiance.

Lemon Tree Hotels represent a fresh blend of both business and leisure offerings in the midscale space, infusing every stay with an invigorating and spirited experience. True to their name, these hotels provide an experience that is both refreshing and zestful.

Upon arrival, guests are greeted with a warm welcome, cheerful smiles and the signature essence of lemon. The bright interiors, infused with playful wit and dynamic energy, ensure a revitalising experience. Thoughtfully designed in-room amenities, vibrant cafés, lively recreation bars, refreshing swimming pools and well-equipped fitness centres deliver a sense of comfort at an exceptional value.

The Indian tourism sector continues on a growth trajectory. In 2024, the sector contributed approximately I21 trillion ($244.53 billion) to India’s GDP, accounting for 6.6% of the economy6/7. With a strong surge in domestic tourism and an increasing number of international visitors, India continues to solidify its position as a global tourism hub8. Government initiatives are transforming the sector, thereby offering expansion opportunities to hotel companies.

As India strengthens its position of being one of the fastest-growing tourism markets globally, Lemon Tree Hotels is well placed to emerge as a homegrown champion with international benchmarks. Our mid-market positioning resonates with the aspirations of India’s rising middle class while also attracting global travellers seeking value-driven yet high-quality experiences. With India poised to become one of the world’s top travel destinations, we see ourselves not just as participants but as contributors to shaping the global hospitality narrative.

The demand for travel in India is being further fuelled by unprecedented improvements in infrastructure and rising consumption. The Government’s emphasis on new airports, expressways and the rollout of high-speed Vande Bharat trains is reshaping connectivity across the country, opening up new destinations and making travel more seamless. At the same time, the aspirations of India’s growing middle class, coupled with rising disposable incomes, are driving demand for leisure, wellness and spiritual tourism. These trends provide Lemon Tree Hotels with a unique opportunity to deepen its presence in both established urban centres and emerging destinations.

It gives me great pleasure to present the fourth Integrated Annual Report (and fifth ESG disclosure) of Lemon Tree Hotels for FY25. This year has been marked by significant growth and transformation. As we continue to build a substantial inventory base, our focus remains on driving sustainable growth alongside operational excellence. Our performance this year reflects the effectiveness of our strategy and our commitment to providing exceptional value to all stakeholders.

In FY25, Lemon Tree Hotels reinforced its status as a leading mid-market hospitality brand in India, consistently ranking among the top five in terms of media visibility. Our share of voice ranged between 3% and 9%5 during the year, peaking with announcements of strategic expansions and sustainability initiatives.

In FY25, we delivered a record revenue of I1,288.4 Crores, reflecting a 20% YoY growth driven by higher room revenue and a strong increase in occupancy and Average Daily Rates (ADR). Our Net EBITDA stood at I636.46 Crores, marking a 20% YoY increase, while Profit After Tax (PAT) reached I243.15 Crores, representing a 34% growth. Operational efficiency across our hotels played a key role in these strong results.

Room occupancy reached 71.7%9, supported by a higher ADR across all our brands, bolstered by product upgrades, renovations and enhanced guest services. We continued our asset-light growth strategy, adding 3,784 new rooms through 61 new openings and signings, bringing our total to 17,116 rooms across 212 hotels in 131 destinations. We are on track to meet our target of 20,000 rooms by CY28.
Strategic Goals and Roadmap – Lemon Tree 2.0

Lemon Tree Hotels, India’s largest and most trusted mid-market hotel chain, is committed to expanding its leadership position through the Lemon Tree 2.0 strategy. Having achieved 85% of its CY28 inventory target nearly three years ahead of schedule, our Company is focused on delivering superior financial performance, accelerating asset-light growth and leveraging digital transformation to enhance guest experiences and improve operational efficiency.
In May 2024, Lemon Tree Hotels celebrated 20 years since opening the first property in Udyog Vihar, Gurugram, marking the first milestone in our growth journey.
10 Source: Meltwater Monthly Comparative report 11 For 41 Owned/Leased hotels 12 Reduction in intensity terms vs. the FY19 baseline

At the heart of our strategy is asset-light growth, with expansion through managed and franchised hotels to maximise scalability with minimal capital investment. We aim to reach a 20,000‑room network by CY28, with more than 70% of our portfolio being asset-light. This will be complemented by retail acceleration to increase direct bookings and reduce reliance on third‑party platforms, while expanding our Infinity Rewards Loyalty Programme to further strengthen customer engagement.

We are also driving digital transformation to leverage Bionic Revenue Management, automate processes and make data-driven decisions in order to improve profitability. To ensure flexibility in our growth while maintaining a strong balance sheet, we will continue to optimise our cost structure and be best-in-class.

Another key component of our strategy is sustainability. Lemon Tree Hotels is committed to achieving 100% green-certified hotels (owned/ leased) by FY26, aligned with global standards for responsible growth. This includes eco-friendly designs, energy-efficient solutions and water recycling and reuse across our properties.

Lemon Tree’s strategy has led to operational gains and increased visibility in industry platforms and in media. This has been reflected in a sustained growth in brand mentions in top publications like The Economic Times and IndianRetailer.com10.

For us, sustainability is not an adjunct but a cornerstone of Lemon Tree 2.0. We firmly believe that our growth must be in harmony with the environment and society. From pioneering energy‑efficient hotels to creating one of the most inclusive workforces in the industry, we view ESG not as a compliance requirement but as a driver of innovation, efficiency and long-term value.

Lemon Tree Hotels is dedicated to becoming a leader in ESG with clear targets for sustainability and diversity. By FY26, our goal is to achieve 30% women on our board, while in the workforce we aim for 15% women and 20% Opportunity-Deprived Individuals (ODIs). Our environmental targets for FY26 include reducing energy intensity by 15%,
water intensity by 10% and GHG intensity by 40% vs. the FY19 baseline. Renewable energy is targeted at 50% and we aim to achieve 100% green-certified buildings (owned/leased properties) by FY26.

In FY25, we made significant progress, certifying 34.15%11 of our hotels as green buildings and reducing water intensity by 13.73%12. Our renewable energy exit rate stood at 27.93% in March 2025, with FY26 set for a significant jump to 50%. Furthermore, 16.4% of our workforce comprises ODIs and 11.69% are women, reflecting our ongoing efforts in diversity and governance.

Looking ahead, we remain committed to achieving asset-light growth through expansion in urban centres and underserved markets. We will continue to leverage digital transformation to enhance operational efficiency, optimise processes and drive profitability. Sustainability remains a core priority, with clear goals for all aspects. We will continue investing in our brands and improving the guest experience while ensuring long-term value creation for our shareholders. With these strategic initiatives, Lemon Tree Hotels is well-positioned to capture future growth opportunities and reinforce its leadership in the mid-market hotel sector.

As we look to the future, our commitment goes beyond growth and profitability. We aspire to be the most inclusive and sustainable hospitality brand in Asia, where technology and humaneness coexist to deliver memorable guest experiences. Our journey is about building not just hotels, but communities of opportunity and impact.

None of this progress would have been possible without the relentless dedication of our people, the trust of our guests and the continued support of our shareholders and partners. I extend my sincere gratitude to each member of the Lemon Tree family who has contributed to our success in a rapidly evolving consumer landscape, often in the face of macroeconomic challenges. Your‑resilience and commitment have enabled us to consistently raise the bar. With the continued trust of our stakeholders, we are confident of shaping a future where Lemon Tree Hotels leads the way in responsible growth.
Chairman and Managing Director Lemon Tree Hotels Limited

The demand for mid‑market hotels is growing rapidly, especially in Tier 2/3 cities, driven by rising domestic tourism and the increasing disposable income of the middle class. These segments are becoming increasingly attractive to those looking for affordable yet high‑quality accommodations.

Government schemes such as Swadesh Darshan and PRASHAD have significantly improved infrastructure, opening up new regions for development. These initiatives are boosting connectivity and creating new tourism opportunities in previously underserved areas.

Sustainability has become a high-level priority for both travellers and the hospitality industry. Ecofriendly practices, from green certifications to systems that are energy and/or water efficient, are now essential factors for both consumers and businesses.

Digital transformation unfolds in two ways. First, by using data to offer travelers personalised packages and boost the Company’s revenue. Second, by adopting digital services like mobile check-ins and smart room controls, which streamline and enhance the guest experience.

The demand for wellness and spiritual tourism is on the rise as more travellers seek enriching experiences focused on health and spiritual growth. This trend is particularly strong in regions with rich spiritual heritage, such as Varanasi, Ayodhya and Rishikesh.

The Indian tourism sector is on a robust growth trajectory, fuelled by both rising domestic and international travel. Government initiatives such as the Swadesh Darshan and PRASHAD Schemes are enhancing infrastructure, further driving the development of tourism across the country. These advancements are creating a number of opportunities for the hospitality industry, especially in the mid-market segment. A growing middle class, higher disposable incomes and a surge in business travel are all contributing to increased demand for affordable, high-quality accommodations. As travellers increasingly seek value-for-money options, Lemon Tree Hotels is well-positioned to capitalise on this expanding market, unlocking potential for growth and market share expansion.

Our Company is strategically expanding its presence in both urban centres and emerging Tier 2 and Tier 3 cities. With 212 hotels and 17,116 rooms, we are on track to reach 20,000 rooms by CY28. Our asset-light model ensures flexibility and scalability, enabling us to expand efficiently through management contracts and franchise agreements.

Digital transformation is reshaping our business in two important ways. First, we are gathering and analysing data to better understand our travellers, enabling us to design personalised packages while maximising overall revenue. Second, we are embracing digital services that continue to reshape the hospitality sector. From mobile check-ins to smart room controls and other intuitive features, we are simplifying and enriching the guest experience at every step.

Wellness tourism and spiritual retreats are emerging as strong growth drivers, and we are well-positioned to tap into this rising demand. Riding this wave, we are adding properties that offer wellness-focused and spiritual experiences, capitalising on the evolving preferences of both domestic and international travellers.

With new regional airports, enhanced highways and tourism hubs coming online, we are capitalising on these infrastructure developments by expanding into regions that benefit from improved connectivity and emerging tourism circuits. This focus enables us to strengthen our portfolio in newly connected and high‑potential markets.

Lemon Tree Hotels has set a clear path towards sustainable growth aiming for 100% green-certified hotels by FY26. In FY25, we have achieved 34.15%13 of our hotels are already green-certified, with another 24% work-in-progress. We have targeted achievement of 50% renewable energy as well as a 15% energy intensity reduction and a 10% water intensity reduction by FY2614. This focus aligns with global trends and consumer demand for eco-friendly travel options, helping Lemon Tree stay competitive, as corporate clients include sustainability criteria in Request for Proposal (RFP) process while shortlisting hotels.

At Lemon Tree Hotels, we have adopted a multi‑pronged approach to improve energy efficiency across our properties. One of the key initiatives has been the installation of a Building Management System (BMS) at Aurika Skycity, Mumbai, which helps optimise energy consumption throughout the building. Additionally, we implemented an energy management system at our properties in Sector 60, Gurugram, which includes two hotels and a convention centre, enabling better control over energy use.

At Lemon Tree Premier and Red Fox Hotel in HITEC City, Hyderabad, we upgraded our fresh air units by retrofitting them with energy-efficient electronic commutator (EC) motors. These motors significantly reduce energy consumption while improving overall ventilation.

In FY25, we replaced all CFL lights with LED lights at our Keys properties, contributing to a substantial reduction in energy consumption. We also transitioned from Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) to Piped Natural Gas (PNG) at Lemon Tree Hotel, Indore, marking a shift to cleaner fuel and further enhancing our sustainability efforts.
Total Energy Consumption within the Organisation in FY25 (GJ)
Fuel-wise Energy Consumption within the Organisation in FY25 (GJ)

We have also installed heat pumps across multiple properties, replacing conventional hot water generators. This switch has significantly reduced the GHG footprint by using electricity more efficiently and lowering overall energy consumption. In FY25, heat pumps were added to properties in Goa, Cochin and Trivandrum.

As part of our upgradation and renovation strategy, FY25 saw key enhancements, including the replacement of air-conditioning units across four properties, upgrades of sewage treatment plants at select locations and the standardisation of lower chiller set points across 20 properties. In addition, Aurika Skycity, Mumbai (669 rooms), operated for the full year, recording a 10 percentage-point occupancy increase compared to the previous year, when it had launched mid-year. These upgrades and higher operational load contributed to a marginal 2.90% rise in energy intensity versus the FY19 baseline.

At the same time, energy efficiency initiatives are underway across 41 owned/leased hotels and are expected to deliver tangible results in the coming years, driving planned reductions in energy intensity relative to the FY19 baseline, consistent with progress achieved in FY24, FY23, FY22 and FY21.

As of FY25, we have increased the number of green-certified buildings to 15, with an additional 19 hotels undergoing evaluation and audits by both our consultants and the Indian Green Building Council (IGBC). The audit results have led to recommendations for further sustainable improvements, including the application of Solar Reflective Index (SRI) paint on rooftops to reduce the building’s heat envelope, updating waste collection and disposal processes, and enhancing fresh air ventilation.

In line with our efforts to reduce the environmental impact of our Diesel Generator (DG) sets, we have implemented dual fuel systems to mitigate air quality concerns and ensure regulatory compliance. In FY25, we installed Dual Fuel Kits (DF Kits) at four additional properties: Lemon Tree Premier and Aurika Skycity in Mumbai, Lemon Tree Premier 2

Renewable energy (RE) adoption is governed by state-specific regulations and our RE agreements across India are planned and executed according to the criteria set by each state government. Over the past few years, navigating the renewable energy landscape has posed challenges, as some states have made it easier to enter RE agreements quickly, while others continue to experience longer approval timelines.
Recognising the potential environmental impacts of our sector and the serious threats climate change poses to economic and sector stability, we actively engage in initiatives to reduce emissions.

Our commitment to sustainability extends beyond emissions reduction to promoting clean energy solutions. In FY25, we installed 77 Electric Vehicle (EV) charging stations across both owned/leased hotels in 7 cities and managed/franchised hotels in 6 cities. This infrastructure includes 15 fast chargers (DC 60 kW dual gun), 5 medium chargers (DC 30 kW single gun), and 57 slow chargers (Type 2 and 2W AC chargers). By providing these facilities, we encourage our guests to shift to electric vehicles, further contributing to the reduction of emissions and promoting cleaner transportation options.

Recognising the growing scarcity of water, we are committed to improving the efficiency of our water usage across all properties. To address this, we have implemented several measures designed to reduce consumption without compromising the guest experience. These include lowering water pressure from the pipes, installing aerators on taps for efficient water flow and replacing older fixtures with water-efficient green alternatives.
We also maximise the use of flow restrictors and dual flush systems, further enhancing our water management efforts.
In addition, we encourage guest participation in conservation through initiatives like our third‑day linen change programme and the half-glass water initiative.

Water consumption across our 41 owned/leased hotels and 26 managed hotels increased by approximately 11% in FY25. This increase was largely driven by the full-year operation of Aurika Skycity, which was only operational for six months in FY24, and a 10% increase in occupancy. With 669 rooms, Aurika Skycity has the largest inventory in India, and its water consumption grew by 32%.

To optimise water use and ensure sustainable resource management, we have implemented various programmes, including recycling, wastewater treatment, rainwater harvesting and water conservation. Additionally, we have run water saving programmes which involve our guests and customers directly.
Reduction in water consumption (intensity- based) compared to FY19 baseline
We continuously work at improving existing systems and installing water-efficient equipment to enhance water efficiency.
We use Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs) for continuous wastewater treatment and recycling.

Through our Half-glass Water Service and Third-day Linen Change programme, we achieve significant water savings while raising awareness among guests. These initiatives have been well supported and appreciated by our customers.
Reduction in water consumption (intensity-based) compared to FY19 baseline38

In 2023, we began transitioning to purified bottled water to reduce plastic consumption across our hotels. The pilot of the Reverse Osmosis (RO) plant was conducted at Lemon Tree Premier and Red Fox Hotel in Aerocity, followed by the installation of a capacitive de-ionisation (CDI) system at Aurika Skycity in Mumbai. In 2025, we installed and commissioned one CDI plant for two hotels, Lemon Tree Premier and Red Fox Hotel in HITEC City, Hyderabad and four RO plants for five hotels, including Lemon Tree Hotel and Red Fox Hotel in Sector 60, Gurugram; Lemon Tree Premier, International Airport, Mumbai; Aurika, Udaipur; and Lemon Tree Premier, Ulsoor Lake, Bengaluru. This phased approach has resulted in a saving of 16,390 kgs of plastic. Additionally, we have observed cost savings of 5-10% at each location.

Our water management practices include a stringent quality control process with Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for cleaning, sanitising, filling and packaging the bottled water. This ensures that the water meets the highest safety and hygiene standards, reflecting our commitment to efficient water management and sustainability.
Despite the increase in occupancy, particularly at our largest hotel, Aurika Skycity, the amount of recycled water has remained consistent with FY24 levels.

Our best-in-class waste management practices focus on reducing, segregating and handling waste efficiently across all hotel facilities, including guest rooms, restaurants, public areas and operational spaces.

To implement focused strategies, we assess waste generation across our hotels, gathering valuable insights that help guide our efforts. Ensuring compliance with environmental regulations set by the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MOEF&CC) is a primary objective for both our upcoming and existing hotels. To raise awareness and facilitate effective implementation of our ESG and Waste Management Policy, we conduct regular training sessions and provide ongoing support to empower our employees.

In FY25, the total waste generated at our 41 owned/leased hotels and 26 managed hotels, as covered in the scope of this report, was 23,33,869 kg vs. 1,368,029 kg in FY24. Hazardous waste generation increased to 133.17 MT, compared to 82.70 MT in FY24. Non-hazardous waste generation also saw an increase, rising to 2,334 MT from 1,461 MT in FY24. The increase in certain waste categories was primarily due to the replacement and upgrade of air conditioning systems in select hotels, alongside a more streamlined approach to waste data monitoring and record-keeping.
Implementing comprehensive practices for waste reduction, segregation and recycling.
Conducting regular training to promote effective waste management practices and enhance employee engagement.

To further enhance water management, we are gradually replacing older Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs). This replacement process began with Lemon Tree Hotel, Chennai, which has shown promising early results. The STP at Aurika Skycity, Mumbai, is equipped with Membrane Bio-Reactor (MBR) technology, combining biological treatment with membrane filtration to produce high-quality treated water.

We accept our responsibility as environmental stewards, as we understand the potential effects our activities and development may have on the local ecosystem and natural resources. Preserving nature, biodiversity and the distinctive environments where our properties are located is our responsibility. As a result, we ensure that our properties do not infringe on areas designated as protected, such as animal reserves and national parks. We are committed to incorporating habitat preservation and biodiversity into all facets of our business operations, growth and acquisitions.
• We eliminated the use of packaged drinking water and single-use plastic. • We played an active role in managing forest fires and contributed by planting over 100 trees.

We are committed to creating a dynamic, supportive work environment that prioritises employee well-being and builds a diverse, inclusive culture. Our people management programmes, including cross-training and upskilling, provide equal opportunities without discrimination. Our Human Rights Policy reflects our commitment to protecting employee rights. We prioritise engagement, health and safety, demonstrating our dedication to a vibrant workplace.

Employee Engagement We follow a flat structure that reduces hierarchies and fosters openness, strengthening collaboration, encouraging participation and building team spirit. We value talent regardless of gender, background or needs. Our strategy aims to attract young talent at various levels, bringing fresh perspectives to drive growth and long‑term development.

Total Employees by Contract Type In FY25, across our 41 owned/leased and 26 managed hotels covered within the scope of this report, our full-time workforce stood at 5,621, compared to 5,470 in FY24, reflecting a 2.76% YoY increase. Contractual employees stood at 924 in FY25, while non-employed workers totalled 2,020. These shifts across full-time, contractual and non-employed categories highlight our capacity to scale operations while remaining agile and responsive to business needs.

In October 2024, we launched an Internship Programme under the Prime Minister’s Internship Scheme (PMIS), initiated by the Ministry of Corporate Affairs. The scheme offers paid internships where a part of the stipend is supported by us and the larger portion is contributed by the government. The objective is to nurture industry-ready talent for the hospitality sector and enable young people to access meaningful job opportunities with greater ease.

Further, we have entered into a memorandum of understanding with various Institutes of Hotel Management (IHM) across multiple cities, under the purview of the Ministry of Tourism. This partnership is designed to create greater awareness about the industry and its dynamics, with activities focused on student engagement, faculty development and institutional development.

Harvard Business School (HBS) conducted a research study jointly with Lemon Tree Hotels to evaluate passion and well-being among employees. Nearly 400 managers in Band 5 and above (Assistant Manager and Above levels) participated in the study, which was conducted over a period of four weeks.

The research focused on how employees can sustain and replenish their passion over time, thereby reducing the risk of burnout. The insights and lessons shared by HBS are now being carefully evaluated and implemented in a phased manner across Lemon Tree.

Diversity and Inclusion Our Diversity and Inclusion Policy reflects our commitment to building a culture of trust, responsibility and long-term socioeconomic development among our employees and within the communities where we operate. For nearly two decades, we have been a focused equal-opportunity employer, actively advancing diversity and inclusion across our workforce.

We strive to provide equitable employment opportunities to Opportunity Deprived Individuals (ODIs), including Employees with Disability (EwDs) and individuals from economically and socially marginalised backgrounds (EcoSocs). By integrating ODIs into our teams, we enable them to lead dignified lives while also fostering meaningful and inclusive guest experiences.
In FY25, across our 41 owned/leased and 26 managed hotels covered in the scope of this report, ODIs accounted for 16.4% of our workforce. Gender diversity stood at 88% male and 12% female employees.

Key Diversity and Inclusion Initiatives Over the last 18 years, building a culture of inclusion and diversity has become a part of the DNA of our Company. We have embedded it across multiple departments within a hotel and across multiple hotels in the city and then across multiple cities.

We have also done trials and internships across multiple disability types including Down Syndrome, Intellectual and Developmental Disability and Autism. The exploration of different departments and bringing in new disability types requires a structured process of observations, followed by job mapping and then planning the training for interns with special needs. It is this flow that allows us to build our base and higher more Employees with Disability.

Supporting Inclusive Employment In FY25, our workforce included 315 Employees with Disability (EwDs) and 605 individuals from economically and socially marginalised backgrounds (EcoSocs) across the 41 owned/leased and 26 managed hotels within the scope of this report.

Learning and Development This year, we focused on refreshing our content, strengthening multiple training modules and building knowledge around our new loyalty programme – Infinity Rewards. We redefined and enhanced the ‘Moments of Truth’ interactions that guests typically experience at Lemon Tree, whether at the Front Desk, in our restaurants or during interactions with Housekeeping. These updates reflect evolving consumer needs and ensure that our processes continue to create memorable guest experiences.

Our sales training content was also realigned to better equip and empower our sales team. The programme has been redesigned from a six-day classroom format to a comprehensive three-month module. It now integrates pre-work, online sessions, classroom learning and case studies, providing a stronger foundation and enabling sales professionals to deliver consistent and improved results.

To support the rollout of Infinity Rewards, we introduced a company-wide training and briefing module. Designed for both online and in-person delivery, it was complemented by a contest developed by the training team to deepen engagement and drive adoption of the programme.

It is our endeavour to identify new opportunities within hotels to onboard individuals with diverse disabilities, including those requiring additional support in training and grooming. To this end, we have strategically re-examined various roles in Front Office, Food & Beverage, Housekeeping, Bell Desk, Finance, and Human Resources.

Short pilot projects have been conducted across different work areas, and we continue to explore roles that are suitable following a structured process of job mapping. These initiatives have been carried out extensively in Delhi/NCR, Mumbai, Bhopal, Bengaluru, and Chennai, in collaboration with Evoluer Solutions, Action for Autism, Sai Swayam, Mann NGO, Yash Charitable Trust, Diya Foundation, and Annabalaya.

Talent Management In August 2024, we introduced the Hotel General Managers (HGMs) Development Programme as a structured three-month initiative to strengthen functional learning and leadership effectiveness. The programme is designed to help HGMs better understand their roles, recognise individual skills and knowledge and align their hotel leadership teams with Lemon Tree’s evolving strategy and vision. Learning is delivered through online sessions conducted by Corporate Function Heads, as well as self-reading, videos, simulations, live cases and case studies.

In FY25, driven by our rapid growth plans, we opened 12 new hotels in India and overseas. This expansion created multiple internal job opportunities for our high-potential employees, enabling them to take on higher responsibilities and move into roles such as Deputy Head of Department or Head of Department at the new properties. Beyond being a strong career advancement strategy, this approach allows us to organically embed Lemon Tree’s culture into new hotels as they open.

Employee Support Employee well-being and security remain a priority for us and we offer comprehensive benefits to support the diverse needs of our workforce. All permanent employees are covered under full accidental and health insurance, while maternity benefits are provided to female employees. In addition, day-care facilities are available for all employees, recognising the importance of enabling work–life balance across different life stages.

In FY25, 97% of employees across the 41 owned/ leased and 26 managed hotels within the scope of this report were covered under Provident Fund (PF). Gratuity coverage rose from 78% in FY24 to 80% in FY25. Employees’ State Insurance (ESI) coverage stood at 60% in FY24, before adjusting to 56% in FY25.

Talent Retention In FY25, the turnover rate across our 41 owned/ leased and 26 managed hotels within the scope of this report was 47%. Retention stood at 42% for male full-time employees and 59% for female full-time employees. Notably, 78% of our new hires during the year were under the age of 30, reflecting our focus on attracting young talent into the organisation.

Health, Safety and Well-being Health and safety are top priorities for us and our Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) Management System covers all employees and hotels. We routinely identify potential work-related hazards, conduct both routine and non-routine risk assessments and are committed to ensuring that no occupational health risks affect our employees, contractors, guests, communities or the public. We aim to achieve zero incidents across all our operations.
All hotels are equipped with automatic smoke detectors, audible alarms, fire extinguishers, hydrants, portable firefighting equipment and emergency lighting to ensure preparedness.

A comprehensive Food Production Manual guides training and procedures across receiving, handling, storing, cooking, reheating and serving food, ensuring that all food and beverages meet the highest safety standards.
We employ trained security personnel, install monitoring and search equipment and maintain standardised response procedures to safeguard employees, guests and visitors.
By hiring locally and sourcing from nearby suppliers, we contribute to local economic development and support community resilience.
Designated trained operators oversee equipment handling, supported by detailed safety procedures and maintenance protocols followed by authorised personnel.
We support employee well-being through health camps, smoking cessation initiatives, dietary counselling, mental health workshops and fitness programmes.
Regular cleaning and sanitization of high-touch surfaces such as doorknobs, countertops, room keys and EDC machines ensure a hygienic environment for all.
Health and safety risks are regularly reviewed and managed through design and engineering controls, preventive maintenance, administrative measures and structured safety training.
New Hires by Age Group for FY25 (%)

Hazard Identification and Mitigation We have implemented an Incident Tracking System that enables us to detect potential hazards early and take proactive measures to mitigate risks. Reported hazards are acted upon immediately, with preventive steps put in place to avoid recurrence.

Employee safety remains central to our approach, supported by comprehensive safety training programmes and well-documented emergency response plans. In addition, regular safety audits and risk assessments actively involve employees in identifying areas for improvement, ensuring hazards are promptly addressed and the risk of incidents minimised.

Health and Safety Governance Our Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) standards, rules and procedures are overseen at the corporate level by our OHS Committee. Each hotel also has a local OHS Committee to ensure compliance with regional regulations. These committees focus on incident analysis and prioritising mitigation actions. In addition, all our hotels maintain trained first-aid personnel on site to provide immediate support in case of medical emergencies, thereby safeguarding the well-being of employees, guests and visitors.

In FY25, 51.29% of our full-time employees across the 41 owned/leased and 26 managed hotels within the scope of this report received training on health and safety measures, including 47.99% male employees and 76.26% female employees. This compares with FY24, when 40% of employees were trained, including 39% male and 48% female employees.

Human Rights We have implemented a Human Rights Policy that sets out clear expectations for employees, business partners and third-party contractors. This policy is aligned with international standards such as the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) and the International Labour Organisation (ILO) Declaration, reinforcing our commitment to uphold human rights as a core value across our operations.

We actively raise awareness to prevent human rights abuse and promote community well-being. Our zero-tolerance approach addresses issues such as sexual harassment, discrimination, child labour and all forms of forced labour or exploitation. We also monitor construction sites to prevent violations, misconduct, unfair treatment and
workplace harassment, including for employees affiliated with associations and unions. Reported violations are thoroughly investigated by our Vigilance Officer to ensure safety and fairness for all.

In FY25, our workforce across the 41 owned/ leased and 26 managed hotels within the scope of this report comprised 5,621 full-time employees. Of these, 108 employees (95 male and 13 female) across the owned/leased hotels were members of trade unions. None of the employees in the 26 managed hotels were part of trade unions or covered by collective bargaining agreements. Overall, 1.92% of our full-time employees are covered under trade unions/collective bargaining agreements.

Our offerings are tailored to individual preferences, whether through family-friendly amenities, seamless business support, or curated event services and leisure activities.
We aim to support marginalised and underserved communities by addressing their basic development needs.

Community Partnerships We collaborate with specialised non-profit organisations to create meaningful opportunities for underprivileged youth and enhance their access to employment. Over the years, we have built longstanding partnerships with the following organisations:

• Aasraa Trust • Action for Ability and Inclusion (AADI) • Action for Autism • Muskaan PAEPID • Noida Deaf Society • Sai Swayam Society • Sarthak Educational Trust • National Centre for Promotion of Employment for Disabled People (NCPEDP) – for disability advocacy
Together, these partnerships draw on more than four decades of collective expertise in supporting local communities and promoting social inclusion.

Environmental Stewardship In alignment with our commitment to environmental responsibility, we undertake several initiatives that promote sustainable practices and community well-being. These include:
• Organising tree plantation drives • Conducting cleanliness activities around our properties • Embedding eco-friendly practices across our operations
Through these efforts, we not only contribute to a cleaner, greener environment but also enhance the experience of our extended-stay guests while fostering sustainability for all.

Delivering Customer Satisfaction We place strong emphasis on online reputation management (ORM) and follow a structured process involving our hotel operations and ORM teams to promptly address any service issues or deficiencies. For us, customer satisfaction is a core priority.
In FY25, our customer base continued to expand across the 41 owned/leased and 26 managed hotels within the scope of this report, with a total of 14,10,860 customers compared to 13,72,871 in FY24.

Building on insights from ORM data, in FY25, we introduced a number of targeted service improvement initiatives. These included product upgrades and renovations across the group, along with re-training programmes for our front‑end staff to further improve and consolidate service standards.

Customer Feedback Management We continuously enhance our services and ensure customer satisfaction by valuing guest feedback and responding with care. Feedback is central to our business philosophy and we encourage guests to share their experiences through multiple channels, including our Guest Satisfaction Tracking System (GSTS), online platforms such as TripAdvisor and social media, as well as online travel agencies (OTAs) like Booking.com and MakeMyTrip.

At Lemon Tree Hotels, we act promptly on guest feedback as part of our commitment to excellence. Our Learning and Development (L&D) team reviews each negative or passive comment across GSTS and online platforms using Root Cause Analysis,
focusing on both people and process-related factors to identify underlying issues. Recurring themes are addressed by refining brand standards to ensure consistently high-quality experiences.

To resolve concerns quickly, the L&D team provides re-briefings and targeted corrective training where required, working closely with hotel operations teams. The voice of our customers guides our journey, helping us to strengthen service quality, build genuine connections and drive continuous growth.

Promoting Sustainability through Guest Participation We actively build awareness around sustainability with our guests and prioritise involving them in our initiatives. Through our communication channels, we share information on our waste management practices and their positive environmental impact.

Our guest engagement programme includes water recycling posters in washrooms and our third-day linen change initiative, which encourages guests to conserve water by opting not to change their linen daily. This empowers travellers to play a meaningful role in preserving this critical resource during their stay.
At our restaurants, the half-glass water initiative has been well received by guests, who often recognise and appreciate these efforts through feedback and social media posts.

Loyalty and Rewards Programme In FY25, we launched Infinity Rewards, our new loyalty programme designed to strengthen guest retention, drive direct bookings and reward repeat customers. Members earn and redeem reward coins whenever they stay at Lemon Tree, making each visit more rewarding and memorable.

The programme features four membership tiers — Silver, Gold, Platinum and Platinum Plus, each offering exclusive benefits. These include discounts on room rates, best price guarantee, free in-room Wi-Fi and, for Platinum and Platinum Plus members, complimentary buffet breakfast. Guests earn reward coins on every rupee spent across room stays, food and beverages and other services. Redemption is simple and seamless, with free stays bookable online at participating Lemon Tree properties.
Since its launch, Infinity Rewards has seen rapid adoption. By the end of March 2025, the programme had grown to 19,95,806 members, reflecting strong guest interest and loyalty to the brand.

We follow a Supplier Code of Conduct that reflects our commitment to a sustainable and ethical supply chain. It requires all vendors, suppliers and service providers to adopt responsible practices and comply with international standards, including the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) and International Labour Organisation (ILO) conventions. The Code covers minimum wages, human rights, health and safety, environmental compliance, labour practices, freedom of association, ethical conduct and transparent business operations.
We prioritise guest participation and actively promote awareness of sustainability.

Local Procurement In FY25, the number of our active suppliers rose to 21,724, compared with 20,366 in FY24, of these, 20,770 were local suppliers, representing approximately 96% of the total.

By prioritising local partnerships, we contribute to community growth, support the local economy and reduce our ecological impact. Engaging local suppliers also enables us to generate employment, offer distinctive products and strengthen connections within the business ecosystem. This approach reflects our commitment to sustainability and inclusive community development.
